title: Topic 24: Boosting Services: Why Players Pay Others to Play Their Games
content:
# Topic 24: Boosting Services: Why Players Pay Others to Play Their Games

## ABSTRACT

In the grand theater of digital achievement, where glory is measured in pixels and prestige is quantified by virtual badges, a peculiar cottage industry has emerged: video game boosting. This paper delves into the fascinating and faintly ludicrous world of players who, faced with the Sisyphean task of climbing the competitive ladder, opt for a more capitalistic approach: paying someone else to do the climbing for them. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the primal fear of being a "noob" to the insatiable thirst for instant gratification that defines our modern era. Through a rigorous, albeit tongue-in-cheek, analysis, we reveal that boosting is more than just a transactional service; it is a window into the very soul of the contemporary consumer, a testament to the enduring power of shortcuts, and a celebration of the glorious absurdity of paying someone to have fun on your behalf.

## INTRODUCTION

The flickering glow of the monitor illuminates a single, tear-streaked face. Our hero, a valiant warrior in the digital realm, has once again been vanquished, their dreams of attaining the coveted "Diamond" rank shattered by a team of infuriatingly skilled teenagers. The climb seems impossible, a vertical cliff face of insurmountable odds. But wait! A glimmer of hope appears in the form of a targeted ad, a siren song promising a swift and effortless ascent to the pinnacle of gaming glory. For a modest fee, a digital messiah, a joystick-wielding sherpa, will carry our hero to the promised land. This, dear reader, is the seductive allure of video game boosting.

Boosting, in its simplest form, is the act of hiring a more skilled player to log into your account and play on your behalf, artificially inflating your rank and unlocking a treasure trove of in-game accolades. It is a practice that exists in the murky gray area between legitimate strategy and outright cheating, a digital-age solution to the age-old problem of not being as good at something as you wish you were. This paper seeks to unravel the enigma of boosting, to understand why a rational human being would willingly pay another to partake in the very activity that is meant to be a source of personal enjoyment. We will embark on a journey through the hallowed halls of academic research, the chaotic forums of online gaming communities, and the bizarre marketplace of digital services to explore the psychological, social, and economic forces that have transformed boosting from a niche practice into a multi-million dollar industry. Prepare to have your notions of achievement, entertainment, and the very nature of fun thoroughly and humorously deconstructed.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The hallowed halls of academia have, for the most part, remained blissfully unaware of the burgeoning boosting economy. While scholars have dedicated countless hours to dissecting the societal impact of *Grand Theft Auto* or the philosophical implications of *The Sims*, the humble booster has been largely ignored, a footnote in the grand narrative of video game studies. However, a few brave souls have dared to venture into this uncharted territory. A 2019 study by the University of Limerick, for instance, heroically estimated the ELO boosting market for just three popular games (*League of Legends*, *Overwatch*, and *Dota 2*) to be a staggering $170 million [1]. This groundbreaking research, which likely involved a great deal of time spent on questionable websites, provides a tantalizing glimpse into the sheer scale of this digital gold rush.

To truly understand the boosting phenomenon, one must look beyond the limited academic literature and delve into the related, and far more extensively studied, world of microtransactions. As Touro University Worldwide so eloquently explains, the psychology behind in-game purchases is a masterclass in manipulation [2]. Game developers, like benevolent puppet masters, pull at our psychological strings, exploiting our primal urges for instant gratification and our deep-seated fear of loss. The 99-cent purchase that saves us from the agony of waiting for our virtual crops to grow is, in essence, a micro-dose of the same drug that the booster provides. Both offer a shortcut, a way to bypass the tedious grind and get straight to the good stuff. The booster, then, can be seen as the ultimate microtransaction, a one-time payment that unlocks the ultimate prize: the illusion of skill.

Furthermore, the rise of boosting is inextricably linked to the evolution of the virtual economy. In the early days of online gaming, virtual items and achievements were, for the most part, worthless in the real world. Today, however, the line between the virtual and the real has become increasingly blurred. A rare in-game item can be worth thousands of dollars, and a high-ranking account can be a valuable asset. In this new reality, boosting is not just about ego; it is an investment, a calculated decision to increase the value of one's digital portfolio. The booster, in this context, is not a cheater but a financial advisor, helping their clients to maximize their virtual returns.

## ANALYSIS

At the heart of the boosting phenomenon lies a complex and often contradictory set of psychological drivers. To the outside observer, paying someone to play a game for you may seem like the height of absurdity. Why, one might ask, would you pay to *not* have fun? But to the player languishing in the lower echelons of the competitive ladder, the logic is crystal clear. It is a logic born of frustration, desire, and a healthy dose of cognitive dissonance.

One of the most powerful psychological forces at play is **loss aversion**, a concept that has been well-documented by psychologists and mercilessly exploited by marketers. The pain of losing is, for many, far greater than the pleasure of winning. In the world of competitive gaming, where every loss is a step backward, a public declaration of one's inadequacy, the fear of failure can be all-consuming. Boosting, in this context, is a form of insurance, a way to protect oneself from the psychological trauma of defeat. By outsourcing the risk to a more skilled player, the account owner can enjoy the fruits of victory without ever having to taste the bitter sting of a loss.

Then there is the siren song of **instant gratification**, a melody that has become the anthem of our on-demand culture. We want our food delivered in 30 minutes, our movies streamed on demand, and our virtual validation served up on a silver platter. The traditional path to gaming glory, a path paved with countless hours of practice, dedication, and soul-crushing defeats, is simply too slow for the modern consumer. Boosting offers a tantalizing alternative, a high-speed elevator to the penthouse of a skyscraper that others are forced to climb one grueling flight of stairs at a time. It is the ultimate life hack, a way to achieve in a matter of days what might otherwise take months or even years.

Of course, we cannot ignore the role of **social status** in the boosting equation. In the digital realm, as in the real world, we are constantly being judged, ranked, and categorized. Our in-game rank is more than just a number; it is a badge of honor, a public declaration of our worth. A high-ranking player is a digital demigod, a being to be admired and emulated. A low-ranking player, on the other hand, is a pariah, a digital leper to be shunned and ridiculed. The desire to escape the lower ranks is not just about personal pride; it is about social survival. Boosting, therefore, is a form of social mobility, a way to buy one's way into the digital elite.

Finally, we must consider the remarkable feats of **cognitive dissonance** that allow players to justify their use of boosting services. To an outsider, paying someone to play a game for you might seem like a clear violation of the spirit of competition. But to the player who has just purchased a boosting package, it is a perfectly reasonable, and even necessary, course of action. They may tell themselves that they are simply "saving time," that they are "too busy" to grind their way up the ladder, or that they are "supporting a small business." They may even convince themselves that they are not really cheating, that they are simply "leveling the playing field." These mental gymnastics, however absurd they may seem, are essential for preserving one's self-image as a legitimate and honorable player.

From an economic perspective, the logic of boosting is surprisingly sound. In a world where time is a finite and increasingly valuable commodity, the decision to pay for a boosting service can be seen as a rational and even savvy investment. For the busy professional who only has a few hours a week to play, the prospect of spending months grinding their way up the ladder is simply untenable. By outsourcing the grind to a booster, they can enjoy the prestige and rewards of a high rank without having to sacrifice their precious free time. It is the ultimate expression of the "time is money" adage, a digital-age solution to the age-old problem of having more money than time.

Furthermore, the boosting industry can be seen as a fascinating case study in the gig economy. Boosters are, in essence, digital mercenaries, skilled warriors who sell their services to the highest bidder. They are the Uber drivers of the gaming world, the TaskRabbits of the virtual realm. They set their own hours, work from the comfort of their own homes, and are paid for their specialized skills. For many, boosting is not just a hobby; it is a full-time job, a way to turn their passion for gaming into a lucrative career. As one booster, known only as "Chausa," so eloquently put it, "I can earn way more doing this than having a regular job" [3]. In a world where traditional employment is becoming increasingly precarious, the appeal of the boosting gig is not hard to understand.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The world of boosting is not just a theoretical construct; it is a thriving, and often bizarre, ecosystem with its own set of heroes, villains, and cautionary tales. To truly understand the boosting phenomenon, one must venture into the wild and untamed frontier of the digital marketplace, a world where virtual dreams are bought and sold on a daily basis.

Our first stop on this whirlwind tour of the boosting underworld is **South Korea**, a country where gaming is not just a pastime but a national obsession. In 2018, the South Korean government, in a move that sent shockwaves through the gaming community, declared boosting a criminal offense [4]. This was not just a slap on the wrist; it was a full-blown declaration of war. Under the new law, anyone caught boosting could face up to two years in prison and a fine of up to $18,000. The message was clear: in South Korea, the sanctity of the virtual ladder is a matter of national importance.

Next, we turn our attention to the world of professional gaming, a world where the stakes are high and the temptation to cheat is ever-present. In 2015, the *League of Legends* community was rocked by a scandal involving **XiaoWeiXiao**, a professional player for the now-defunct Team Impulse. XiaoWeiXiao, a player at the top of his game, was caught boosting another player's account for the paltry sum of $1,300 [5]. The fallout was swift and severe. XiaoWeiXiao was suspended from professional play, his reputation was tarnished, and the incident served as a stark reminder that even the most skilled players are not immune to the siren song of a quick buck.

But what of the boosters themselves, the unsung heroes and digital mercenaries who make the whole system work? For every high-profile scandal, there are thousands of anonymous boosters quietly plying their trade, grinding their way through the lower ranks for a modest fee. Their stories, though often untold, are just as fascinating as those of the players they serve. Take, for example, the aforementioned "Chausa," a competitive *League of Legends* player from Europe who has turned boosting into a full-time career [3]. For Chausa, boosting is not just about the money; it is about the freedom and flexibility that it provides. It is a way to escape the drudgery of a traditional 9-to-5 job and to make a living doing what he loves.

Of course, not all boosting stories have a happy ending. For every satisfied customer, there is a cautionary tale of a boosting transaction gone wrong. There are stories of boosters who, after being paid, simply disappear into the digital ether, leaving their clients with a lighter wallet and a broken heart. There are stories of accounts being stolen, of credit card information being compromised, and of players being banned from their favorite games. The world of boosting, like the Wild West of old, is a lawless frontier, a place where fortunes can be made and lost in the blink of an eye.

## DISCUSSION

The rise of boosting services forces us to confront some uncomfortable questions about the nature of achievement, the value of entertainment, and the future of consumerism. In a world where we can pay someone to do just about anything for us, from assembling our furniture to walking our dogs, is it really so surprising that we can also pay someone to play our video games?

At its core, the boosting phenomenon challenges our traditional notions of what it means to "earn" something. Is an achievement that has been bought any less valid than an achievement that has been earned through countless hours of hard work and dedication? In the digital realm, where the lines between the real and the virtual are becoming increasingly blurred, the answer is not as simple as it might seem. For some, the act of playing the game is secondary to the act of possessing the rewards. The destination, in other words, is more important than the journey.

Furthermore, the boosting industry is a fascinating reflection of broader trends in consumer behavior. We live in an age of instant gratification, a world where we have been conditioned to expect immediate results. We want our food fast, our entertainment on-demand, and our virtual validation delivered with the click of a button. Boosting is simply the logical extension of this trend, a service that caters to our insatiable desire for a quick and easy fix. It is a testament to our willingness to trade time for money, to outsource the tedious and to pay a premium for convenience.

The future of boosting is, like the future of so many things in our rapidly changing world, uncertain. As game developers become more sophisticated in their methods of detection, and as the penalties for boosting become more severe, the industry may be forced to adapt or die. We may see a rise in "coaching" services, where players pay for one-on-one instruction rather than for direct account access. We may see the emergence of new and more subtle forms of boosting, services that are harder to detect and even harder to regulate. Or, perhaps, we will see a cultural shift, a rejection of the win-at-all-costs mentality that has fueled the boosting industry and a renewed appreciation for the simple joy of playing the game, win or lose.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, pixelated tapestry of human endeavor, the existence of video game boosting services is a thread of pure, unadulterated absurdity. It is a testament to our species’ boundless ingenuity, our unwavering commitment to finding the path of least resistance, and our profound ability to turn even the most trivial of pursuits into a complex economic ecosystem. We have journeyed through the psychological depths of the gamer’s mind, navigated the treacherous waters of the digital marketplace, and emerged with a simple, yet profound, conclusion: people will pay for just about anything, especially if it makes them look good in front of their virtual friends.

So, the next time you see a player with an impossibly high rank and a suspiciously low skill level, do not be too quick to judge. They may not be a cheater; they may simply be a savvy consumer, a patron of the digital arts, a connoisseur of the outsourced victory. They are, in their own way, a hero of our time, a living embodiment of the glorious, and slightly pathetic, human desire to have our cake and pay someone else to eat it for us.
references:
[1] Conroy, E., & O'Reilly, G. (2021). Boosting: Rank and skill deception in esports. Computers in Human Behavior, 119, 106718.
[2] Touro University Worldwide. (2016, February 25). Insert More Coins | The Psychology Behind Microtransactions. https://www.tuw.edu/psychology/psychology-behind-microtransactions/
[3] Esports Heaven. (2017, February 2). Boosted: A Story of boosters and why they do what they do. https://www.esportsheaven.com/features/boosted-a-story-of-boosters-and-why-they-do-what-they-do/
[4] Wikipedia. (n.d.). Boosting (video games). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boosting_(video_games)
[5] Ibid.
